+def _check_condition(condition, src_fs, src_path, dst_fs, dst_path):
+    # type: (Text, FS, Text, FS, Text) -> bool
+    """Check if a copy condition is met."""
+    if condition == "newer":
+        if not dst_fs.exists(dst_path):
+            return True
+        src_mtime = src_fs.getinfo(src_path, ["details"]).modified
+        dst_mtime = dst_fs.getinfo(dst_path, ["details"]).modified
+        if src_mtime is None or dst_mtime is None:
+            return True
+        return src_mtime > dst_mtime
+    elif condition == "older":
+        if not dst_fs.exists(dst_path):
+            return True
+        src_mtime = src_fs.getinfo(src_path, ["details"]).modified
+        dst_mtime = dst_fs.getinfo(dst_path, ["details"]).modified
+        if src_mtime is None or dst_mtime is None:
+            return True
+        return src_mtime < dst_mtime
+    elif condition == "exists":
+        return dst_fs.exists(dst_path)
+    elif condition == "not_exists":
+        return not dst_fs.exists(dst_path)

requirements.description:
The mirror() function currently accepts (src_fs, dst_fs, walker=None, copy_if_newer=True, workers=0)
      but does NOT accept a preserve_time keyword argument. All 32 tests in TestMirror_0/1/2/3 call
      mirror(..., preserve_time=True) and fail with:
        TypeError: mirror() got an unexpected keyword argument 'preserve_time'
requirements.contract:
Add a preserve_time=False boolean parameter to mirror(). When preserve_time=True, after copying
      each file from src_fs to dst_fs, the modification time (mtime) of the destination file must be
      set to exactly match the source file's modification time (obtained via getinfo with "details"
      namespace, then settimes on the destination). The _mirror() helper should likewise accept and
      propagate this parameter. The existing copy_if_newer behavior is unaffected when preserve_time
      is False (the default). No change to the external signature for existing callers is permitted.

      Full updated signature:
        mirror(src_fs, dst_fs, walker=None, copy_if_newer=True, workers=0, preserve_time=False) -> None

requirements.location: code/fs/copy.py — new module-level functions copy_file_if(), copy_dir_if(), copy_fs_if()
requirements.description:
The fs.copy module does not expose copy_file_if, copy_dir_if, or copy_fs_if. Approximately
      30 tests across TestCopyIfNewer, TestCopyIfOlder, TestCopyIfExists, and TestCopyIfNotExists call
      these functions and fail with:
        AttributeError: module 'fs.copy' has no attribute 'copy_file_if' (or copy_dir_if / copy_fs_if)
requirements.contract:
Add the following three functions to code/fs/copy.py:

      copy_file_if(src_fs, src_path, dst_fs, dst_path, condition) -> bool
        Copies the file at src_path to dst_path according to condition. Returns True if the copy
        was performed, False otherwise.

      copy_dir_if(src_fs, src_path, dst_fs, dst_path, condition,
                  walker=None, on_copy=None, workers=0) -> None
        Copies all files from the src directory to dst directory for which the condition is met.
        on_copy, walker, and workers behave identically to copy_dir().

      copy_fs_if(src_fs, dst_fs, condition, walker=None, on_copy=None, workers=0) -> None
        Equivalent to copy_dir_if(src_fs, "/", dst_fs, "/", condition, ...).

      The condition parameter is a string with one of the following values:
        "newer"     — copy file if src does not exist in dst, OR src.mtime > dst.mtime
        "older"     — copy file if src does not exist in dst, OR src.mtime < dst.mtime
        "exists"    — copy file ONLY if the corresponding dst file already exists
        "not_exists"— copy file ONLY if the corresponding dst file does NOT exist

      For "newer" and "older", if modification times are unavailable (None), perform the copy.
      For same-filesystem copies, use FS.copy() with overwrite=True when the condition is met.
      For cross-filesystem copies, use copy_file_internal() via the existing Copier/locking pattern.
      An unsupported condition value should raise ValueError.

requirements.description:
copy_dir() and copy_fs() do not accept a preserve_time keyword argument. The 9 tests in
      TestCopySimple (test_copy_dir_0, test_copy_dir_1..3, test_copy_dir0, test_copy_fs_0..3) call
      them with preserve_time=True and fail with:
        TypeError: copy_dir() got an unexpected keyword argument 'preserve_time'
        TypeError: copy_fs() got an unexpected keyword argument 'preserve_time'
requirements.contract:
Add preserve_time=False to the signatures of copy_dir() and copy_fs():

        copy_dir(src_fs, src_path, dst_fs, dst_path, walker=None, on_copy=None,
                 workers=0, preserve_time=False) -> None
        copy_fs(src_fs, dst_fs, walker=None, on_copy=None, workers=0,
                preserve_time=False) -> None

      When preserve_time=True, after each file is successfully copied to dst, the modification time
      of the destination file is set to the source file's modification time using dst_fs.settimes().
      The source mtime is retrieved with src_fs.getinfo(src_path, ["details"]).modified.
      If the source mtime is None, skip the settimes call for that file.
      copy_fs() must forward preserve_time to copy_dir().

requirements.description:
(a) move_file(), move_dir(), move_fs() do not accept a preserve_time keyword argument.
          6 tests in TestMoveCheckTime_0 and TestMoveCheckTime_1 fail with:
            TypeError: move_file() / move_dir() / move_fs() got an unexpected keyword argument 'preserve_time'
      (b) move_file() does not accept a cleanup_dst_on_error keyword argument. 2 tests
          (test_move_file_cleanup_on_error_0, _1) fail with:
            TypeError: move_file() got an unexpected keyword argument 'cleanup_dst_on_error'
      (c) move_file() with a read-only source does not clean up the destination file on error.
          2 tests (test_move_file_read_only_mem_source, test_move_file_same_fs_read_only_source)
          fail because the file is copied to dst even though src.remove() raises ResourceReadOnly.
requirements.contract:
Update the three move functions with the following new signatures:

        move_file(src_fs, src_path, dst_fs, dst_path,
                  preserve_time=False, cleanup_dst_on_error=True) -> None
        move_dir(src_fs, src_path, dst_fs, dst_path,
                 workers=0, preserve_time=False) -> None
        move_fs(src_fs, dst_fs, workers=0, preserve_time=False) -> None

      preserve_time behavior: when True, after moving each file, the mtime of the destination is
      set to match the original source mtime (retrieved before the copy).

      cleanup_dst_on_error behavior for move_file(): if any exception is raised after the copy
      step but before (or during) the removal of the source (e.g., ResourceReadOnly from
      src.remove()), AND cleanup_dst_on_error=True, then the destination file must be deleted
      (if it exists) before re-raising the exception. When cleanup_dst_on_error=False, the
      destination file is left in place on error.

      move_dir() and move_fs() must forward preserve_time to the underlying copy_dir() call.

requirements.description:
(a) The FS.copy() instance method and its overrides (OSFS.copy, WrapFS.copy) do not accept
          a preserve_time parameter. Tests test_copy_preserve_time in TestOSFS (test_osfs.py,
          test_subfs.py, test_tempfs.py) and TestSubFS (test_subfs.py) fail with:
            TypeError: OSFS.copy() got an unexpected keyword argument 'preserve_time'
            TypeError: WrapFS.copy() got an unexpected keyword argument 'preserve_time'
      (b) FS.getmodified() does not exist. TestMemoryFS::test_copy_preserve_time (test_memoryfs.py)
          calls self.fs.getmodified("foo/file.txt") and fails with:
            AttributeError: 'MemoryFS' object has no attribute 'getmodified'
requirements.contract:
(a) Add preserve_time=False to FS.copy() in base.py, and to any overriding copy() methods
          in osfs.py and wrapfs.py. When preserve_time=True, after copying the file contents,
          retrieve the source file's modification time via getinfo(src_path, ["details"]).modified
          and call settimes(dst_path, modified=src_mtime) on the same filesystem (since copy() is
          an intra-filesystem operation). If src_mtime is None, skip the settimes call.

      (b) Add getmodified(path) -> Optional[datetime] to FS in base.py as a convenience method.
          It must return the modification datetime of the resource at path, equivalent to:
            self.getinfo(path, namespaces=["details"]).modified
          It should raise ResourceNotFound if path does not exist (propagated from getinfo).

requirements.location: fs/opener/ftpfs.py — class FTPOpener (method open_fs) and module-level protocols list
requirements.description:
FTPOpener currently only declares protocols = ["ftp"] and never passes a tls parameter
      when constructing FTPFS. As a result:
      (a) opening a URL with the "ftps" scheme raises UnsupportedProtocol because no opener
          handles that protocol;
      (b) even for plain "ftp" URLs, FTPFS is constructed without a tls keyword argument,
          causing mock-based tests to fail because they expect tls=False in the call signature.
requirements.contract:
- FTPOpener.protocols must include both "ftp" and "ftps".
      - open_fs must determine whether TLS is requested by inspecting the protocol of the
        parsed URL (i.e., "ftps" → tls=True, "ftp" → tls=False).
      - FTPFS must be constructed with the keyword argument tls set to the determined boolean
        value, in addition to all existing parameters (host, port, user, passwd, proxy, timeout).
      - The method signature and return type contract are unchanged.
      - For "ftps" URLs, FTPFS is instantiated with tls=True; for "ftp" URLs, with tls=False.

requirements.description:
WrapReadOnly overrides individual write operations (remove, removedir, setinfo, copy,
      makedir, etc.) to raise ResourceReadOnly, but it does NOT override removetree.
      Consequently, a call to removetree on a WrapReadOnly instance falls through to the
      WrapFS base class implementation in wrapfs.py, which delegates to the underlying
      filesystem and actually performs the deletion instead of raising ResourceReadOnly.
      This causes test_removetree to receive ResourceNotFound (the underlying fs has no
      "foo" directory) and test_move_dir / test_move_fs to silently succeed where they
      should fail.
requirements.contract:
- WrapReadOnly must provide a removetree(self, dir_path) method.
      - The method must raise fs.errors.ResourceReadOnly(dir_path) unconditionally,
        without delegating to the wrapped filesystem.
      - Signature: removetree(self, dir_path: Text) -> None
      - The behaviour must be consistent with all other write-blocking methods in WrapReadOnly.

requirements.location: fs/_ftp_parse.py — module-level RE_LINUX regex constant and decode_linux function
requirements.description:
RE_LINUX has two gaps that cause valid FTP LIST lines to be silently skipped or
      parsed with missing fields:
      1. The permissions group pattern [ldrwx-]{10} does not accept the sticky-bit character
         't' (e.g. "drwxr-xr-t") or the setuid/setgid character 's' (e.g. "drwsr-xr-x"),
         so lines with these permission strings yield no match and are dropped entirely.
      2. The user and group capture groups use [\w\-]+ which excludes '.' (dot), '$' (dollar),
         and '@' (at-sign); usernames/groups such as "foo.user$" or "foo@group_" are therefore
         not matched, causing those entries to be dropped from the parsed result.
requirements.contract:
- The permissions capture group must accept any character that may legally appear in a
        Unix permission string of length 10, including 's' (setuid/setgid) and 't' (sticky bit)
        in addition to 'l', 'd', 'r', 'w', 'x', and '-'.
      - The user and group capture groups must accept all characters that commonly appear in
        Unix usernames and group names: alphanumeric characters, '-', '_', '.', '$', and '@'
        at minimum.
      - The decode_linux function is not required to change its logic; the fix is confined to
        broadening the RE_LINUX pattern so that previously-unmatched lines now produce a match.
      - All lines that previously matched must continue to match and produce identical output.

requirements.location: fs/wrap.py — class WrapCachedDir, methods isdir and isfile
requirements.description:
WrapCachedDir.isdir and WrapCachedDir.isfile both delegate to self.getinfo(path).
      getinfo raises ResourceNotFound when the path does not exist in the cached directory
      listing. Because isdir and isfile do not catch this exception, querying a non-existent
      path raises ResourceNotFound instead of returning False, violating the FS interface
      contract (isdir and isfile must return a bool, never raise for absent paths).
      The source code even contains FIXME comments acknowledging this bug.
requirements.contract:
- WrapCachedDir.isdir(path) must return False when the resource does not exist,
        instead of propagating ResourceNotFound.
      - WrapCachedDir.isfile(path) must return False when the resource does not exist,
        instead of propagating ResourceNotFound.
      - When the resource does exist, isdir must return True if and only if it is a directory,
        and isfile must return True if and only if it is a file (existing behaviour preserved).
      - Signature: isdir(self, path: Text) -> bool  /  isfile(self, path: Text) -> bool

requirements.description:
error_tools.py constructs PathError (and its subclasses) using
      fserror(self._path, exc=exc_value). PathError.__init__ currently only accepts
      (self, path, msg=None) and does not accept an exc keyword argument, so this call
      raises TypeError: PathError.__init__() got an unexpected keyword argument 'exc'.
      In contrast, ResourceError.__init__ already accepts exc as an optional parameter.
      PathError must be brought into parity so that exc can be supplied and stored,
      enabling error_tools.py to attach the originating OS exception to path-level errors.
requirements.contract:
- PathError.__init__ must accept an optional exc keyword argument
        (type: Optional[Exception], default: None).
      - The exc value must be stored as self.exc on the instance.
      - The existing path and msg parameters and their semantics are unchanged.
      - __reduce__ must be updated to include exc so that pickling round-trips correctly:
        it should return (type(self), (self.path, self.exc, self._msg)) or equivalent.
      - Subclasses of PathError that do not override __init__ (e.g. NoSysPath, InvalidPath)
        must automatically inherit the new exc parameter without further changes.
      - Signature: __init__(self, path: Text, exc: Optional[Exception] = None, msg: Optional[Text] = None) -> None

requirements.description:
Two test methods — `test_removetree_root` and `test_move_dir_same_fs` — are entirely absent
      from `FSTestCases`. Because all concrete filesystem test classes (TestOSFS, TestMemoryFS,
      TestTarFS, TestMountFS, TestMultiFS, TestZipFS, TestSubFS, TestTempFS, TestAppFS variants,
      etc.) inherit from `FSTestCases`, the absence of these two methods causes ~37 tests to be
      reported as "not collected". They must be added to `FSTestCases` so every inheriting class
      automatically gets them.
requirements.contract:
1. `test_removetree_root`: Populate `self.fs` with at least one subdirectory and one file,
         then call `self.fs.removetree("/")`. After the call:
         - `self.fs.listdir("/")` must return an empty list (all content is gone).
         - The filesystem itself must still be open and operational (e.g., capable of creating new
           directories and files without error).
         - The root directory "/" must still exist as a directory.

      2. `test_move_dir_same_fs`: Populate `self.fs` with a directory tree rooted at `"foo"` that
         contains nested subdirectories and at least one file with known text content. Call
         `fs.move.move_dir(self.fs, "foo", self.fs, "foo2")` — both the source and destination
         filesystem objects are the same instance (`self.fs`). After the call:
         - The source path `"foo"` must no longer exist on `self.fs`.
         - A new path `"foo2"` must exist with the same directory structure and file content that
           `"foo"` previously contained.
         - Files that existed at the root level of `self.fs` but outside `"foo"` must be unaffected.
         Both assertions must be made using the existing `self.assert_text`, `self.assert_exists`,
         `self.assert_not_exists`, and/or `walk.walk_dirs` helpers already present in `FSTestCases`.

requirements.location:
fs/info.py, docstrings of: Info.get (line ~98), Info.suffix (line ~188),
              Info.suffixes (line ~205), Info.stem (line ~221)
requirements.description:
The doctest examples for four `Info` instance methods reference a variable named `info`, but
      this variable is never defined within those docstrings. The doctest harness does not inject
      an `info` binding into globals, so every access fails with:
          NameError: name 'info' is not defined. Did you mean: 'Info'?

      For `fs.info`, the harness injects `my_fs` — a MemoryFS pre-populated with:
        - `foo.tar.gz`  (empty file)
        - `foo.py`      (file with text content)
        - `bar/`        (directory)
requirements.contract:
Each affected docstring must be updated so that `info` is defined before it is used.
      The first line of each example must retrieve an `Info` object from `my_fs`:

      - `Info.get`: requires an `Info` object that carries the `'access'` namespace.
        Setup example: `info = my_fs.getinfo('foo.py', namespaces=['access'])`.
        The expected return value of `info.get('access', 'permissions')` must match what
        `MemoryFS` returns for a file's access permissions, or use the `...` ELLIPSIS
        placeholder (the harness enables `doctest.ELLIPSIS`) so any non-raising result is accepted.

      - `Info.suffix`: requires an `Info` object for a file with a single extension.
        Setup: `info = my_fs.getinfo('foo.py')`.
        Expected result of `info.suffix`: `'.py'` (deterministic, no ELLIPSIS needed).

      - `Info.suffixes`: requires an `Info` object for a file with multiple extensions.
        Setup: `info = my_fs.getinfo('foo.tar.gz')`.
        Expected result of `info.suffixes`: `['.tar', '.gz']` (deterministic).

      - `Info.stem`: requires the same multi-extension setup as `Info.suffixes`.
        Setup: `info = my_fs.getinfo('foo.tar.gz')`.
        Expected result of `info.stem`: `'foo'` (deterministic).

      The `>>> info` display line in each docstring (showing the repr of the object, e.g.,
      `<info 'foo.py'>`) must either be replaced with the correct Info repr or omitted if it
      would produce an implementation-specific string that is hard to assert exactly.

        - `bar.py` (text file with 3 lines)
        - `bar.pyc` (empty binary file)

      - `Globber.count`: replace the example so it calls `my_fs.glob('**/*.py').count()`.
        The expected return value must match a MemoryFS containing exactly those two `.py` files:
        `Counts(files=2, directories=0, data=...)` with `...` for `data` (ELLIPSIS accepted).

      - `Globber.count_lines`: replace the example so it calls
        `my_fs.glob('**/*.py').count_lines()`. The expected return value must reflect the actual
        line counts of `foo.py` (1 line) and `bar.py` (3 lines):
        `LineCounts(lines=4, non_blank=3)`.

      - `Globber.remove`: replace the example so it calls
        `my_fs.glob('**/*.pyc').remove()`. The expected return value must reflect the two `.pyc`
        files present: `2`.

      No `import fs` or `open_fs` call should remain in any of these three doctest examples.

requirements.description:
The docstring example for FS.download (and by inheritance FS.getfile) calls
      my_fs.download('/movies/starwars.mov', write_file). However, the test harness
      constructs my_fs for the fs.base module with a file at 'Videos/starwars.mov'
      (not 'movies/starwars.mov'). As a result, the doctest raises
      fs.errors.ResourceNotFound instead of completing successfully.
requirements.contract:
The docstring example in FS.download must reference a path that exists in the
      mock filesystem provided during doctest execution. For the fs.base module, the
      mock filesystem contains a file at the path '/Videos/starwars.mov'. The example
      must use this path (or another path that is guaranteed to exist in the mock). The
      example should open a writable binary file-like object and call download without
      raising an exception, producing no output (the method returns None).
      Signature context: download(self, path, file, chunk_size=None, **options) -> None.

requirements.description:
The docstring for the Permissions class contains the example:
          oct(p.mode)
      with expected output '0764'. In Python 3, the built-in oct() returns a string
      prefixed with '0o' (e.g., '0o764'), not the Python 2 octal literal format
      '0764'. The doctest therefore fails because the actual output is '0o764'.
requirements.contract:
The docstring example for the Permissions class must show the correct Python 3
      output for oct(p.mode). Given p = Permissions(user='rwx', group='rw-', other='r--'),
      oct(p.mode) must be expected to return '0o764' (the Python 3 octal string
      representation). The surrounding context (creation of p and the print(p) line) can
      remain unchanged.

requirements.description:
The docstring for FS.lock contains the example:
          >>> with my_fs.lock():  # May block
          ...    # code here has exclusive access to the filesystem
      A with-block whose body consists solely of a comment is invalid Python syntax.
      The Python interpreter raises IndentationError: expected an indented block after
      'with' statement on line 1, causing the doctest to fail.
requirements.contract:
The docstring example for FS.lock must contain a syntactically valid Python
      with-block. The body must include at least one executable statement (e.g., a pass
      statement) so that Python accepts it as a valid indented block. The comment
      explaining exclusive access may be retained as an inline or standalone comment
      alongside the executable statement, but the executable statement is required.
      The example must complete without raising any exception.

requirements.description:
The docstring for normpath includes an expected exception example, but the
      Traceback block is malformed in two ways:
      1. The header line reads "Traceback (most recent call last)" without the required
         trailing colon. Python's doctest module only recognises an expected exception
         when the header is exactly "Traceback (most recent call last):" (with colon).
         Without the colon, doctest does not treat the block as an expected exception,
         so the actual raised exception appears as an unexpected failure.
      2. The expected exception message ends with a spurious double-quote character
         (filesystem") that does not appear in the actual exception message.
      Additionally, the exception class shown as "IllegalBackReference" should be the
      fully-qualified name "fs.errors.IllegalBackReference" to match what Python prints
      in the traceback.
requirements.contract:
The docstring example for normpath("foo/../../bar") must follow the standard
      Python doctest exception format exactly:
        - The header line must be "Traceback (most recent call last):" (colon required).
        - The ellipsis line "..." must be present to skip the traceback body
          (compatible with doctest.ELLIPSIS flag used by the test suite).
        - The final exception line must match the actual exception string raised by the
          implementation, specifically:
              fs.errors.IllegalBackReference: path 'foo/../../bar' contains
              back-references outside of filesystem
          (no trailing double-quote).
      The behaviour of normpath itself must not be changed; the fix is limited to
      correcting the docstring format.

requirements.description:
The docstring examples for FS.match use a variable named home_fs:
          >>> home_fs.match(['*.py'], '__init__.py')
          True
          >>> home_fs.match(['*.jpg', '*.png'], 'foo.gif')
          False
      The variable home_fs is not injected into the doctest globals by the test harness
      (test_doctest.py only injects my_fs, os, fs, open, OSFS, print_list, pprint, and
      time). Consequently both examples raise NameError: name 'home_fs' is not defined.
requirements.contract:
The docstring examples for FS.match must use a variable that is available in the
      doctest execution context. The test harness provides my_fs (a MemoryFS instance)
      under that exact name. Replace all occurrences of home_fs in the docstring
      examples with my_fs. The expected return values (True for a matching pattern,
      False for a non-matching pattern) must remain correct and consistent with the
      behaviour of FS.match on any valid FS instance. The behaviour of FS.match itself
      must not be changed; the fix is limited to the docstring.

requirements.description:
The docstring of make_repr contains a self-contradictory example. The example defines
      a __repr__ method that always passes the string literal 'foo' as a positional argument
      to make_repr, yet the doctest asserts that MyClass(None) renders as MyClass() (with no
      arguments at all). Because the current implementation unconditionally includes every
      positional argument in the output, the call make_repr('MyClass', 'foo', name=(None, None))
      produces MyClass('foo') instead of the expected MyClass(). The test runner
      (tests/test_doctest.py::TestDoctest::test_fs__repr_make_repr) executes this doctest
      verbatim and fails on that assertion.
requirements.contract:
The docstring example in make_repr must be internally consistent and must pass when
      executed as a doctest. The example must demonstrate:
        (a) that keyword arguments whose value equals their default are omitted from the repr, AND
        (b) that when all kwargs equal their defaults the resulting repr contains only the
            class name followed by empty parentheses, i.e. "ClassName()".
      The example class definition, the __repr__ body, and all expected-output lines must be
      mutually consistent so that every >>> expression produces exactly the annotated output.
      The make_repr function signature and its filtering semantics for keyword arguments
      ((value, default) tuples omitted when value == default) must remain unchanged.
      Only the docstring example (the lines starting with >>> / ...) may be updated;
      the implementation logic of make_repr itself need not change.

requirements.description:
The module-level docstring of wrap.py demonstrates the read_only wrapper with:
          projects_fs = open_fs('~/projects')
      During doctest execution, open_fs is replaced by the mock _open_fs defined in
      tests/test_doctest.py. That mock resolves osfs://~ (the home directory) but does not
      resolve osfs://~/projects because the resource portion of the parsed URL is not exactly
      '~'. As a result it raises RuntimeError("not allowed in doctests: osfs://~/projects"),
      which causes all subsequent lines to fail with NameError (read_only_projects_fs is never
      defined). The test runner (tests/test_doctest.py::TestDoctest::test_fs_wrap) therefore
      fails.
requirements.contract:
The module-level docstring of wrap.py must contain a self-contained doctest that:
        (a) opens a filesystem using a URI scheme that the doctest mock allows. Permitted
            schemes are: mem://, temp://, ftp://, ftps://, or osfs://~ (the bare home dir).
        (b) wraps it with read_only() to produce a read-only filesystem object.
        (c) demonstrates that calling .remove() on the read-only wrapper raises
            fs.errors.ResourceReadOnly, using the standard Traceback (most recent call last):
            ... ellipsis form accepted by doctest.ELLIPSIS.
      The doctest must execute without touching the real OS filesystem and must not import
      any symbol that is not already available in the module's namespace or in the doctest
      globals provided by the test harness (fs, open_fs via mock, read_only).

requirements.description:
The _has_drive_letter function has a prose docstring that describes which paths return
      True (e.g. "D:/Data", "C:\\My Documents\\test") and which return False (e.g.
      "/tmp/abc:test"), but contains no executable doctest examples (no >>> lines).
      The test tests/test_doctest.py::TestDoctest::test_fs__url_tools__has_drive_letter is
      expected to pass in the target state but is not collected in the current run because
      there is no doctest to load from this function.
requirements.contract:
The docstring of _has_drive_letter must include at least two doctest examples using
      >>> syntax:
        - At least one call that returns True (a path with a Windows-style drive letter
          followed by / or \, e.g. 'D:/Data' or 'C:\\path').
        - At least one call that returns False (a path without a drive letter prefix, e.g.
          '/tmp/abc').
      The examples must be runnable without any imports beyond what is already in the
      function's module scope, and must not rely on OS state or filesystem side effects.
      The existing prose description may be retained; the doctest examples should be added
      within or appended to the existing docstring.
requirements.acceptance_tests:
tests/test_doctest.py::TestDoctest::test_fs__url_tools__has_drive_letter must be
      collected and must pass (exit code 0).

requirements.description:
The decode_windowsnt function has a prose docstring describing the Windows NT FTP LIST
      line format it decodes, but contains no executable doctest examples (no >>> lines).
      The test tests/test_doctest.py::TestDoctest::test_fs__ftp_parse_decode_windowsnt is
      expected to pass in the target state but is not collected in the current run.
requirements.contract:
The docstring of decode_windowsnt must include at least one doctest example using
      >>> syntax that:
        (a) Constructs a valid Windows NT FTP LIST line string (e.g. a directory entry
            such as '11-02-18  02:12PM       <DIR>          images').
        (b) Obtains a match object by calling RE_WINDOWSNT.match() on that string
            (RE_WINDOWSNT is defined at module scope and will be available in doctest globals
            via the test harness's dict(**module.__dict__) glob injection).
        (c) Calls decode_windowsnt(line, match) and verifies at least one field of the
            returned dict, such as result['basic']['name'] or result['basic']['is_dir'].
      The examples must not perform network I/O and must not rely on OS filesystem state.
      All referenced names (RE_WINDOWSNT, decode_windowsnt) are available in the doctest
      execution context through the module's own namespace.
requirements.acceptance_tests:
tests/test_doctest.py::TestDoctest::test_fs__ftp_parse_decode_windowsnt must be
      collected and must pass (exit code 0).

requirements.description:
The removetree method has a prose docstring but contains no executable doctest examples
      (no >>> lines). The test tests/test_doctest.py::TestDoctest::test_fs_base_FS_removetree
      is expected to pass in the target state but is not collected in the current run.
requirements.contract:
The docstring of FS.removetree must include at least one doctest example using >>>
      syntax that:
        (a) Uses the my_fs fixture that the test harness injects into the doctest globals
            for the fs.base module. For fs.base, my_fs is a MemoryFS pre-populated with:
            'Desktop/' directory, 'Videos/' directory, 'Videos/starwars.mov' file, 'file.txt'
            file.
        (b) Calls my_fs.removetree() on an existing directory (e.g. 'Videos').
        (c) Verifies the outcome, for example by asserting my_fs.isdir('Videos') is False
            or my_fs.exists('Videos') is False.
      The example must not perform real filesystem I/O; my_fs is an in-memory filesystem
      provided by the test harness and requires no additional setup in the docstring itself.
requirements.acceptance_tests:
tests/test_doctest.py::TestDoctest::test_fs_base_FS_removetree must be collected and
      must pass (exit code 0).

requirements.description:
The method `Info.is_writeable(namespace, key)` currently has a complete implementation
      but its docstring lacks an `Example:` section with executable doctest code.
      As a result, the doctest machinery in `tests/test_doctest.py` cannot generate the
      test case `TestDoctest::test_fs_info_Info_is_writeable`, causing it to be missing
      from the collected test suite even though the target implementation expects it to pass.
requirements.contract:
Add a doctest `Example:` block to the `is_writeable` method's docstring.
      The example must:
      - Use `my_fs` (a MemoryFS pre-populated for the `fs.info` module with files
        including `foo.tar.gz`, `foo.py`, and directory `bar`) to retrieve file info.
      - Retrieve info with one or more namespaces (e.g., `'access'`) via `my_fs.getinfo(...)`.
      - Call `info.is_writeable(namespace, key)` on the resulting `Info` object with a
        namespace and key that are known to be non-writable (i.e., not in the `_write`
        list for that namespace), expecting the return value `False`.
      - Alternatively, demonstrate a key that IS writable, expecting `True`.
      - The example must be self-contained and produce deterministic output within the
        mocked test environment (where `open_fs` is patched but `my_fs` is a real MemoryFS).
      - Must NOT import anything extra; `my_fs` is already available in the doctest globals.
requirements.acceptance_tests:
tests/test_doctest.py::TestDoctest::test_fs_info_Info_is_writeable
      — This test method must be collected and pass (exit code 0).

requirements.description:
The `TempFS` class docstring describes arguments and behavior but contains no
      `Example:` section with executable doctests. The target report records a passing
      test `TestDoctest::test_fs_tempfs_TempFS`, which can only exist if a doctest is
      present in the class-level docstring. Currently this test is entirely absent from
      the collected suite.
requirements.contract:
Add an `Example:` block to the `TempFS` class docstring that demonstrates:
      - Creating a `TempFS` instance (using the constructor directly or via `open_fs`).
      - Verifying at least one observable property of the instance (e.g., its `repr`,
        whether it is a filesystem object, or basic FS operations such as listing the
        root directory).
      - The example must produce deterministic, verifiable output.
      - In the test environment, `open_fs('temp://')` returns a `MemoryFS()`; if the
        example uses `open_fs`, the result should be treated accordingly.
      - The example must clean up or be side-effect-free (TempFS instances created
        directly will create real temp directories, so prefer `open_fs('temp://')` if
        cleanup is a concern, or ensure any TempFS opened in the example is properly
        closed).
requirements.acceptance_tests:
tests/test_doctest.py::TestDoctest::test_fs_tempfs_TempFS
      — This test method must be collected and pass (exit code 0).

requirements.description:
The `TempFS.close()` method has no docstring at all — only the implementation body.
      The target report records a passing test `TestDoctest::test_fs_tempfs_TempFS_close`,
      which requires a doctest to exist in the `close` method's docstring. Currently this
      test is entirely absent from the collected suite.
requirements.contract:
Add a docstring to the `TempFS.close()` method that includes:
      - A brief prose description of what `close()` does (triggers auto-clean if
        `auto_clean=True`, then closes the underlying OSFS).
      - An `Example:` block demonstrating:
        * Obtaining a TempFS (directly or via `open_fs('temp://')`).
        * Calling `.close()` on it without error.
        * Optionally verifying post-close state (e.g., `tmp_fs.isclosed()` returns `True`).
      - The example must produce deterministic output and be side-effect-free in the
        test environment.
requirements.acceptance_tests:
tests/test_doctest.py::TestDoctest::test_fs_tempfs_TempFS_close
      — This test method must be collected and pass (exit code 0).

requirements.description:
The `FTPFS` class docstring describes its arguments but provides no `Example:`
      section with executable doctests. The target report records a passing test
      `TestDoctest::test_fs_ftpfs_FTPFS`, which requires a doctest in the class-level
      docstring. Currently this test is entirely absent from the collected suite.
requirements.contract:
Add an `Example:` block to the `FTPFS` class docstring that demonstrates:
      - Creating or obtaining an FTPFS instance. In the test environment, `fs.ftpfs.FTPFS`
        is fully mocked via `mock.patch.object(fs.ftpfs, "FTPFS")`, so any call to
        `FTPFS(...)` will return a Mock object — the example must produce output that
        is valid even with a mock return value.
      - Alternatively, the example may use `open_fs('ftp://...')` which also returns
        a `MemoryFS()` in the mocked test environment, and demonstrate basic FS
        operations on the result (e.g., `listdir('/')`).
      - The example must produce deterministic, verifiable output that is compatible
        with the mocked environment.
      - Must NOT make real network connections.
requirements.acceptance_tests:
tests/test_doctest.py::TestDoctest::test_fs_ftpfs_FTPFS
      — This test method must be collected and pass (exit code 0).
